name: Social Psychology
description: An exploration of how individuals' thoughts, feelings, and behaviors are influenced by the real, imagined, or implied presence of others.

modules.title: Introduction to Social Psychology
modules.objectives: Define social psychology, describe its scope and methods, and distinguish it from sociology and personality psychology.
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Intro to Social Psychology
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Social Psychology Intro Practice
modules.assignment: Describe a personal experience where the presence of others changed your behavior or emotions. Analyze the experience using a social psychological concept introduced in this module (e.g., social facilitation, social norms).

modules.title: Social Cognition
modules.objectives: Explain how people think about others, describe attribution theory, identify cognitive biases in social judgment, and apply schema theory to social perception.
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Attribution Theory & Fundamental Attribution Error
modules.assignment: Observe two social interactions (real life or in a film). For each, write a brief attribution analysis: what internal and external attributions might observers make, and what biases might affect those attributions?

modules.title: Self & Identity
modules.objectives: Describe self-concept, self-esteem, and self-efficacy; explain self-serving bias and social comparison theory; analyze how culture shapes self-construal.
modules.assignment: Write a 300-word personal essay analyzing your self-concept using Tajfel's Social Identity Theory. Identify two social groups you belong to and explain how membership in each influences your identity.

modules.title: Attitudes & Attitude Change
modules.objectives: Define attitudes and their components (ABC model), explain cognitive dissonance theory, and describe how attitudes relate to behavior.
modules.assignment: Describe a personal experience of cognitive dissonance — a time when your actions conflicted with your beliefs. Explain how you resolved the dissonance and connect your experience to Festinger's theory.

modules.title: Persuasion
modules.objectives: Explain the Elaboration Likelihood Model (ELM), distinguish central from peripheral routes to persuasion, and evaluate the effectiveness of persuasion techniques.
modules.video2Note: YouTube — The Science of Persuasion (Cialdini's Principles)
modules.assignment: Analyze a real advertisement using the Elaboration Likelihood Model. Identify whether the ad primarily uses central or peripheral route persuasion, list specific techniques used, and evaluate its likely effectiveness for different audiences.

modules.title: Social Influence & Conformity
modules.objectives: Describe Asch's conformity studies, distinguish normative from informational social influence, and explain the factors that increase or decrease conformity.
modules.assignment: Describe a real-life situation where you or someone you observed conformed to a group. Identify whether normative or informational social influence was at play, and analyze what factors increased or decreased the likelihood of conformity.

modules.title: Obedience
modules.objectives: Summarize Milgram's obedience experiments and their findings, identify situational factors that promote obedience, and discuss ethical debates surrounding the research.
modules.assignment: Write a 350-word critical analysis of Milgram's obedience experiment. Address: (1) the key findings, (2) two situational factors that affected obedience, (3) one major ethical concern, and (4) what the study reveals about human nature.

modules.title: Group Dynamics
modules.objectives: Explain social loafing, deindividuation, groupthink, and group polarization, and analyze how group structure influences decision making and performance.
modules.assignment: Analyze a historical or contemporary example of groupthink (e.g., Bay of Pigs, Challenger disaster, or a business failure). Identify at least three symptoms of groupthink and propose two strategies that could have prevented it.

modules.title: Prosocial Behavior & Altruism
modules.titleZh: 亲社会行为与利他主义
modules.objectives: Explain the bystander effect and diffusion of responsibility, compare evolutionary and social learning explanations of altruism, and identify factors promoting helping behavior.
modules.video2Note: YouTube — The Bystander Effect & Kitty Genovese
modules.assignment: Describe a scenario where you or someone you know helped or failed to help someone in need. Analyze the decision to help (or not) using Latané and Darley's decision model and the bystander effect.

modules.title: Aggression
modules.objectives: Define aggression, compare biological and social learning explanations, evaluate the frustration-aggression hypothesis, and assess the influence of media on aggression.
modules.assignment: Evaluate the frustration-aggression hypothesis using two real-world examples. Discuss the role of biological, cognitive, and situational factors in each example, then propose one evidence-based strategy to reduce aggression in each context.

modules.title: Prejudice & Discrimination
modules.objectives: Distinguish prejudice, stereotyping, and discrimination, explain in-group/out-group dynamics, and evaluate strategies for reducing prejudice.
modules.assignment: Analyze the Jane Elliott 'Blue Eyes / Brown Eyes' exercise using social psychological concepts: in-group bias, stereotyping, and the contact hypothesis. What does the exercise demonstrate and what are its limitations?

modules.title: Interpersonal Attraction
modules.objectives: Identify psychological factors influencing attraction (proximity, similarity, physical attractiveness, reciprocity), and explain the mere exposure effect.
modules.assignment: Apply at least three psychological principles of attraction to analyze a real friendship or relationship you know well. Identify which factors were most important in the formation of that relationship and provide specific evidence.

modules.title: Close Relationships
modules.objectives: Compare Sternberg's triangular theory of love, explain attachment styles in adult relationships, and describe social exchange theory as applied to relationship satisfaction.
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Sternberg's Triangular Theory of Love
modules.assignment: Using Sternberg's triangular theory of love, map out three different types of relationships you have observed (e.g., companionate, passionate, consummate love). Explain what each component (intimacy, passion, commitment) contributes to each relationship.

modules.title: Applied Social Psychology
modules.objectives: Apply social psychological principles to real-world domains including health behavior, legal decision making, organizational settings, and environmental sustainability.
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Social Psychology in Everyday Life
modules.assignment: Design a social psychology-based public health campaign to increase recycling or another sustainable behavior. Identify three specific social psychological principles your campaign uses and justify each choice with reference to research.

quizQuestions.question: Social psychology is defined as the scientific study of how people's _____ are influenced by others.
quizQuestions.answer: Thoughts, feelings, and behaviors
quizQuestions.explanation: Social psychology examines how the actual, imagined, or implied presence of others influences thoughts, feelings, and behaviors.

quizQuestions.question: Social facilitation refers to improved performance on tasks in the presence of others when the task is:
quizQuestions.answer: Well-learned and simple
quizQuestions.explanation: The presence of others increases arousal, which improves performance on easy, well-practiced tasks but hurts performance on difficult or new tasks.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following distinguishes social psychology from personality psychology?
options: Social psychology focuses on individual traits; personality focuses on situations
Social psychology emphasizes situational influences; personality emphasizes individual differences
Social psychology studies groups only; personality studies individuals only
There is no meaningful distinction between the two fields
quizQuestions.answer: Social psychology emphasizes situational influences; personality emphasizes individual differences
quizQuestions.explanation: Social psychology focuses on how situations and social contexts shape behavior, while personality psychology focuses on stable individual traits.

quizQuestions.question: The Milgram and Zimbardo studies are important in social psychology because they demonstrated the power of:
quizQuestions.answer: Situational factors in controlling behavior
quizQuestions.explanation: Both studies showed that ordinary people can behave in extreme ways when placed in powerful situational contexts.

quizQuestions.question: Social norms are best described as:
options: Shared rules or expectations for appropriate behavior within a group
quizQuestions.answer: Shared rules or expectations for appropriate behavior within a group
quizQuestions.explanation: Social norms are the unwritten rules that specify how members of a group are expected to behave in given situations.

quizQuestions.question: The fundamental attribution error involves:
options: Overestimating situational causes of others' behavior
Overestimating dispositional causes of others' behavior
Underestimating the importance of our own actions
quizQuestions.answer: Overestimating dispositional causes of others' behavior
quizQuestions.explanation: The FAE is the tendency to attribute others' behavior to stable internal characteristics while underweighting situational factors.

quizQuestions.question: A schema in social cognition is:
options: A emotional reaction to a person or situation
A mental framework that organizes and interprets information
quizQuestions.answer: A mental framework that organizes and interprets information
quizQuestions.explanation: Schemas are organized knowledge structures that shape how we perceive, remember, and interpret new social information.

quizQuestions.question: The actor-observer bias predicts that we attribute our own behavior mostly to _____ and others' behavior to _____.
quizQuestions.answer: Situations; dispositions
quizQuestions.explanation: We tend to explain our own behavior in terms of situational factors and others' behavior in terms of their character (dispositional factors).

quizQuestions.question: Confirmation bias in social cognition means people:
options: Change their beliefs when given new evidence
Seek information that confirms existing beliefs and ignore contradicting evidence
Underestimate the influence of their own biases
quizQuestions.answer: Seek information that confirms existing beliefs and ignore contradicting evidence
quizQuestions.explanation: Confirmation bias is the tendency to search for and favor information that is consistent with one's preexisting beliefs.

quizQuestions.question: Internal attribution explains behavior in terms of:
options: The person's character, ability, or effort
quizQuestions.answer: The person's character, ability, or effort
quizQuestions.explanation: Internal (dispositional) attributions locate the cause of behavior within the person — their personality, ability, or motivation.

quizQuestions.question: Self-efficacy refers to one's belief in:
options: One's ability to succeed at specific tasks
quizQuestions.answer: One's ability to succeed at specific tasks
quizQuestions.explanation: Self-efficacy (Bandura) is the belief in one's capability to perform specific behaviors or reach specific goals.

quizQuestions.question: The self-serving bias involves:
options: Attributing success to external factors and failure to internal factors
Attributing success to internal factors and failure to external factors
quizQuestions.answer: Attributing success to internal factors and failure to external factors
quizQuestions.explanation: The self-serving bias protects self-esteem by attributing successes to our own abilities and failures to situational factors.

quizQuestions.question: Tajfel's Social Identity Theory proposes that self-esteem is partly derived from:
quizQuestions.answer: Membership in valued social groups
quizQuestions.explanation: Social Identity Theory holds that we derive part of our self-concept and self-esteem from the groups we belong to.

quizQuestions.question: Downward social comparison involves comparing oneself to:
options: People who are better off than oneself
People who are similar to oneself
People who are worse off than oneself
quizQuestions.answer: People who are worse off than oneself
quizQuestions.explanation: Downward social comparison to people doing worse helps boost self-esteem, while upward comparison to those doing better can reduce it.

quizQuestions.question: Independent self-construal (common in Western cultures) emphasizes:
options: Relationships, roles, and belonging to groups
Individual autonomy, uniqueness, and personal goals
quizQuestions.answer: Individual autonomy, uniqueness, and personal goals
quizQuestions.explanation: Independent self-construal views the self as separate and autonomous, emphasizing personal attributes and individual goals.

quizQuestions.question: The ABC model of attitudes refers to:
quizQuestions.answer: Affect, Behavior, Cognition
quizQuestions.explanation: Attitudes consist of three components: the affective (emotional), behavioral (action tendency), and cognitive (belief) components.

quizQuestions.question: Cognitive dissonance occurs when:
options: Two attitudes are consistent with each other
An attitude conflicts with an observable behavior
A person holds no strong attitudes
quizQuestions.answer: An attitude conflicts with an observable behavior
quizQuestions.explanation: Cognitive dissonance is the discomfort experienced when a person's actions are inconsistent with their beliefs, motivating attitude change to restore consistency.

quizQuestions.question: Implicit attitudes are:
options: Attitudes we are consciously aware of and can report
Automatic, unconscious evaluations that influence behavior without awareness
quizQuestions.answer: Automatic, unconscious evaluations that influence behavior without awareness
quizQuestions.explanation: Implicit attitudes operate automatically and unconsciously, often measured by reaction time tasks like the Implicit Association Test (IAT).

quizQuestions.question: According to cognitive dissonance theory, a smoker who believes smoking is harmful is most likely to:
options: Reduce dissonance by rationalizing (e.g., 'I'll quit when I'm older')
quizQuestions.answer: Reduce dissonance by rationalizing (e.g., 'I'll quit when I'm older')
quizQuestions.explanation: People reduce cognitive dissonance by changing attitudes, changing behavior, or rationalizing — rationalization is a common strategy.

quizQuestions.question: Attitudes predict behavior best when:
options: Attitudes are implicit rather than explicit
Attitudes are specific to the behavior and strongly held
There is situational pressure to behave otherwise
The attitude was formed by observing others
quizQuestions.answer: Attitudes are specific to the behavior and strongly held
quizQuestions.explanation: Specific, strong, consciously accessible attitudes formed through direct experience are most predictive of corresponding behavior.

quizQuestions.question: The central route to persuasion in the ELM involves:
options: Peripheral cues like attractiveness of the speaker
Careful evaluation of the message's arguments and evidence
Emotional appeals unrelated to the message content
quizQuestions.answer: Careful evaluation of the message's arguments and evidence
quizQuestions.explanation: The central route involves active, thoughtful processing of the quality of arguments — it produces durable attitude change.

quizQuestions.question: The foot-in-the-door technique works because:
options: People feel obligated to repay favors
Agreeing to small requests increases compliance with larger ones
Rejection of an initial request increases guilt
quizQuestions.answer: Agreeing to small requests increases compliance with larger ones
quizQuestions.explanation: Foot-in-the-door exploits self-perception: after agreeing to a small request, people view themselves as cooperative and comply with larger follow-up requests.

quizQuestions.question: Cialdini's principle of 'scarcity' persuades people because:
options: People fear missing out on limited resources (reactance)
Scarcity reduces cognitive load in decision making
quizQuestions.answer: People fear missing out on limited resources (reactance)
quizQuestions.explanation: Scarcity triggers psychological reactance — the motivation to protect one's freedom to have something when it is perceived as limited or disappearing.

quizQuestions.question: The peripheral route to persuasion produces attitude change that is:
options: Strong and resistant to further change
quizQuestions.answer: Temporary and susceptible to counter-persuasion
quizQuestions.explanation: Peripheral route attitude change is based on superficial cues and is therefore weaker and less resistant to future persuasive attempts.

quizQuestions.question: The door-in-the-face technique involves:
options: Starting with a small request and escalating
Making a large request first, then a smaller one
quizQuestions.answer: Making a large request first, then a smaller one
quizQuestions.explanation: Door-in-the-face exploits reciprocal concession: after refusing a large request, people feel obligated to comply with a smaller follow-up request.

quizQuestions.question: In Asch's conformity experiments, participants gave clearly wrong answers approximately what percentage of the time (across trials)?
quizQuestions.explanation: Asch's participants conformed to the incorrect group answer on approximately 37% of the critical trials.

quizQuestions.question: Normative social influence refers to conforming in order to:
options: Be liked and accepted by others
quizQuestions.answer: Be liked and accepted by others
quizQuestions.explanation: Normative social influence is conformity driven by the desire for social approval and fear of rejection.

quizQuestions.question: Informational social influence is most likely in situations that are:
quizQuestions.answer: Ambiguous and uncertain
quizQuestions.explanation: In ambiguous situations where the correct answer is unclear, people turn to others as sources of information to guide their behavior.

quizQuestions.question: Having even one ally who also dissents from the majority _____ conformity in Asch-type experiments.
quizQuestions.answer: Greatly reduces
quizQuestions.explanation: Asch found that the presence of even one social supporter dramatically reduced conformity, breaking the unanimous majority effect.

quizQuestions.question: The tendency to change behavior to be consistent with established social roles is best illustrated by:
quizQuestions.answer: The Zimbardo Stanford Prison Experiment
quizQuestions.explanation: Zimbardo's study showed how powerfully assigned social roles (prisoner, guard) shaped participants' behavior, illustrating role conformity.

quizQuestions.question: In Milgram's original experiment, approximately what percentage of participants delivered the maximum shock level (450V)?
quizQuestions.explanation: About 65% of Milgram's participants delivered the maximum shock level when instructed by the authority figure.

quizQuestions.question: Milgram's study showed that obedience decreased when:
options: The learner was in another room
The authority figure was present in the room
The study was conducted at Yale University
quizQuestions.answer: The experimenter left the room
quizQuestions.explanation: When the experimenter was absent and gave orders by phone, obedience dropped substantially, showing the importance of authority's physical presence.

quizQuestions.question: Which factor increased obedience in Milgram's variations?
options: Moving the learner into the same room as the teacher
Moving the experiment to a run-down office building
Having two other 'teachers' who refused to continue
Having the authority figure leave the room
quizQuestions.answer: Moving the experiment to a run-down office building
quizQuestions.explanation: Milgram found that moving the study away from Yale reduced obedience somewhat, but it remained high, showing authority's power even without institutional prestige.

quizQuestions.question: Agentic state, proposed by Milgram, refers to a psychological state in which:
options: People act as autonomous moral agents
People see themselves as instruments of authority rather than responsible agents
People are in a state of learned helplessness
People follow group norms without reflection
quizQuestions.answer: People see themselves as instruments of authority rather than responsible agents
quizQuestions.explanation: In the agentic state, people shift responsibility upward to authority figures, reducing their own sense of moral accountability.

quizQuestions.question: The main ethical criticism of Milgram's experiments was:
options: Participants were exposed to significant psychological distress
quizQuestions.answer: Participants were exposed to significant psychological distress
quizQuestions.explanation: Many participants showed extreme distress during the study. Critics argued this caused lasting psychological harm and that proper debriefing was insufficient.

quizQuestions.question: Social loafing refers to the tendency to:
options: Work harder in groups than alone
Exert less effort in a group when individual contributions are not identifiable
Conform to group norms regarding work pace
Feel anxiety when working in public settings
quizQuestions.answer: Exert less effort in a group when individual contributions are not identifiable
quizQuestions.explanation: Social loafing occurs when people free-ride in groups because their individual contribution is not identifiable or evaluated.

quizQuestions.question: Group polarization refers to the tendency for groups to:
options: Shift toward more extreme positions after discussion
Follow the opinion of the majority regardless of group discussion
quizQuestions.answer: Shift toward more extreme positions after discussion
quizQuestions.explanation: Group polarization is the finding that group discussion tends to amplify the dominant initial leaning of the group members.

quizQuestions.question: Groupthink is most likely to occur when a group:
options: Is highly cohesive and under pressure for consensus
Has a clearly dominant dissenting voice
quizQuestions.answer: Is highly cohesive and under pressure for consensus
quizQuestions.explanation: Groupthink thrives in highly cohesive groups that prioritize harmony over critical thinking, often under external pressure.

quizQuestions.question: Deindividuation in a crowd is most likely to result in:
options: Decreased inhibitions and increased impulsive behavior
quizQuestions.answer: Decreased inhibitions and increased impulsive behavior
quizQuestions.explanation: Deindividuation — loss of identity in a crowd — reduces self-monitoring and moral restraint, facilitating behaviors people would normally inhibit.

quizQuestions.question: A symptom of groupthink is the illusion of:
quizQuestions.answer: Invulnerability and unanimity
quizQuestions.explanation: Groupthink creates illusions of invulnerability (overconfidence in decisions) and unanimity (suppression of doubt), leading to poor decision making.

quizQuestions.question: The bystander effect predicts that the likelihood of helping decreases as the number of bystanders:
quizQuestions.answer: Increases
quizQuestions.explanation: The bystander effect: the more people present at an emergency, the less likely any individual is to help, due to diffusion of responsibility.

quizQuestions.question: Diffusion of responsibility in a group emergency means:
options: Each person feels more responsible to act
Responsibility is distributed, so each person feels less personally responsible
Larger groups are less likely to notice emergencies
More bystanders means better coordination of help
quizQuestions.answer: Responsibility is distributed, so each person feels less personally responsible
quizQuestions.explanation: When multiple bystanders are present, the sense of personal responsibility to help is diluted across all observers.

quizQuestions.question: Kin selection as an evolutionary explanation of altruism suggests that we are most likely to help:
options: People who are similar to us in attitudes
Those who have helped us before
quizQuestions.answer: Genetic relatives
quizQuestions.explanation: Kin selection proposes that altruism evolved because helping genetic relatives increases the likelihood that shared genes are passed on.

quizQuestions.question: Latané and Darley's model of bystander intervention includes all of the following steps EXCEPT:
options: Interpreting the event as an emergency
Calculating the statistical probability of harm
quizQuestions.answer: Calculating the statistical probability of harm
quizQuestions.explanation: Latané and Darley's model includes: noticing, interpreting, assuming responsibility, knowing how to help, and implementing help — not statistical calculation.

quizQuestions.question: Reciprocal altruism in evolutionary theory refers to helping others:
options: Only when it is genetically beneficial
With the expectation that future help will be returned
When there is an audience watching
quizQuestions.answer: With the expectation that future help will be returned
quizQuestions.explanation: Reciprocal altruism is a strategy in which helping others is evolutionarily advantageous because they are likely to return the favor in the future.

quizQuestions.question: The frustration-aggression hypothesis proposes that aggression is always preceded by:
quizQuestions.answer: Frustration
quizQuestions.explanation: The original frustration-aggression hypothesis (Dollard et al.) proposed that all aggression stems from frustration and all frustration leads to aggression.

quizQuestions.question: Instrumental aggression is:
options: Aggression driven by strong negative emotion
Aggression used as a means to achieve a goal
Aggression provoked by a direct attack
quizQuestions.answer: Aggression used as a means to achieve a goal
quizQuestions.explanation: Instrumental aggression is purposeful and goal-directed — the aggressor uses it as a tool to achieve something, not simply as an emotional reaction.

quizQuestions.question: Bandura's social learning theory explains aggression as primarily the result of:
quizQuestions.answer: Observing and imitating aggressive models
quizQuestions.explanation: Bandura's Bobo doll experiments showed that children learn aggressive behavior by observing and imitating models, even without direct reinforcement.

quizQuestions.question: High ambient temperature has been linked to higher rates of aggression, which supports the:
quizQuestions.answer: Arousal-affect model (General Aggression Model)
quizQuestions.explanation: The General Aggression Model proposes that heat increases physiological arousal and negative affect, both of which prime aggressive cognitions and behavior.

quizQuestions.question: Catharsis as a strategy to reduce aggression:
options: Is strongly supported by current research
Is largely unsupported; venting aggression often increases it
Is effective when violence is observed rather than performed
quizQuestions.answer: Is largely unsupported; venting aggression often increases it
quizQuestions.explanation: Research does not support the catharsis hypothesis — expressing aggression through venting or violent media typically increases rather than decreases aggressive behavior.

quizQuestions.question: A stereotype is:
options: A negative attitude toward a group
An oversimplified generalization about a social group
Differential treatment based on group membership
An unconscious evaluation of a group
quizQuestions.answer: An oversimplified generalization about a social group
quizQuestions.explanation: Stereotypes are cognitive generalizations about the characteristics of members of a social group; they may or may not be accompanied by negative affect (prejudice).

quizQuestions.question: In-group bias refers to the tendency to:
options: Treat all groups equally regardless of membership
Favor members of one's own group over out-groups
Discriminate against in-group members when they deviate from norms
Be more critical of in-group than out-group behavior
quizQuestions.answer: Favor members of one's own group over out-groups
quizQuestions.explanation: In-group bias involves positive evaluation and preferential treatment of one's own group members compared to out-group members.

quizQuestions.question: Discrimination differs from prejudice in that discrimination involves:
options: Actual differential behavior toward group members
quizQuestions.answer: Actual differential behavior toward group members
quizQuestions.explanation: Prejudice is an attitudinal bias; discrimination is behavior — treating people differently based on their group membership.

quizQuestions.question: The contact hypothesis proposes that prejudice can be reduced by:
options: Contact with out-group members under specific positive conditions
quizQuestions.answer: Contact with out-group members under specific positive conditions
quizQuestions.explanation: Allport's contact hypothesis: prejudice decreases when equal-status groups interact cooperatively under institutional support toward common goals.

quizQuestions.question: Scapegoating involves:
options: Fairly attributing blame to those responsible
Blaming a convenient or powerless target for problems caused by others
Using prejudice to motivate in-group achievement
quizQuestions.answer: Blaming a convenient or powerless target for problems caused by others
quizQuestions.explanation: Scapegoating redirects frustration and blame toward a vulnerable group that is not actually responsible for the problems people face.

quizQuestions.question: The mere exposure effect refers to the finding that:
options: We tend to like things more the more we are exposed to them
Attractive people are judged as more familiar
Physical proximity is the only factor in attraction
quizQuestions.answer: We tend to like things more the more we are exposed to them
quizQuestions.explanation: Mere exposure (Zajonc) shows that repeated, non-threatening exposure to a stimulus increases liking of that stimulus.

quizQuestions.question: Similarity in attraction research means people are more attracted to those who:
options: Are physically very different from themselves
Share similar attitudes, values, and interests
quizQuestions.answer: Share similar attitudes, values, and interests
quizQuestions.explanation: Similarity is a robust predictor of attraction — we tend to like people who hold similar attitudes, values, and beliefs to our own.

quizQuestions.question: Physical attractiveness affects attraction primarily because it activates:
options: The 'what is beautiful is good' halo effect
quizQuestions.answer: The 'what is beautiful is good' halo effect
quizQuestions.explanation: The halo effect leads us to assume that physically attractive people also possess positive personality traits, intelligence, and social skills.

quizQuestions.question: Proximity (physical nearness) increases attraction primarily because it:
options: Increases mere exposure and availability for interaction
quizQuestions.answer: Increases mere exposure and availability for interaction
quizQuestions.explanation: Proximity increases attraction by maximizing the opportunities for exposure and interaction — the two strongest drivers of relationship formation.

quizQuestions.question: Reciprocity in attraction refers to the tendency to like people who:
quizQuestions.answer: Like us in return
quizQuestions.explanation: Reciprocity of liking is a powerful predictor of attraction — knowing that someone likes us tends to increase our liking of that person.

quizQuestions.question: According to Sternberg's triangular theory, 'consummate love' involves:
quizQuestions.answer: Intimacy, passion, and commitment
quizQuestions.explanation: Consummate love, the ideal complete love, includes all three components: emotional intimacy, physical passion, and long-term commitment.

quizQuestions.question: According to attachment theory applied to adults, a securely attached adult is likely to:
options: Fear abandonment and be clingy in relationships
Be emotionally distant and avoid intimacy
Trust partners and feel comfortable with intimacy and independence
quizQuestions.answer: Trust partners and feel comfortable with intimacy and independence
quizQuestions.explanation: Securely attached adults feel comfortable with closeness and are not overly worried about abandonment or intimacy.

quizQuestions.question: Social exchange theory predicts that relationship satisfaction depends on:
options: The absolute level of rewards received
Rewards minus costs, compared to one's expectations and alternatives
quizQuestions.answer: Rewards minus costs, compared to one's expectations and alternatives
quizQuestions.explanation: Social exchange theory: satisfaction = (rewards - costs), evaluated relative to comparison level (expectations) and comparison level for alternatives.

quizQuestions.question: Companionate love is characterized by:
options: Deep affection, commitment, and intimacy without intense passion
Passion and commitment without emotional intimacy
quizQuestions.answer: Deep affection, commitment, and intimacy without intense passion
quizQuestions.explanation: Companionate love is the warm, stable affection found in long-term relationships characterized by deep attachment and commitment.

quizQuestions.question: Equity theory in close relationships proposes that people are most satisfied when:
options: They receive more than they give
They give more than they receive
The ratio of contributions and rewards is balanced between partners
quizQuestions.answer: The ratio of contributions and rewards is balanced between partners
quizQuestions.explanation: Equity theory holds that satisfaction is highest when both partners perceive that their contributions and rewards are proportionally balanced.

quizQuestions.question: Social psychological research has found that patient compliance with medical regimens improves when healthcare providers emphasize:
options: Loss framing and concrete, near-term benefits
quizQuestions.answer: Loss framing and concrete, near-term benefits
quizQuestions.explanation: Prospect theory and health psychology research show that framing messages in terms of what is lost by not complying tends to increase health behavior change.

quizQuestions.question: Jury decision making is influenced by the halo effect when:
options: Attractive defendants receive more lenient judgments
quizQuestions.answer: Attractive defendants receive more lenient judgments
quizQuestions.explanation: Research on legal decision making shows that physically attractive defendants tend to receive more favorable verdicts due to the halo effect.

quizQuestions.question: Default options in choice architecture are effective because they exploit:
options: The status quo bias and loss aversion
quizQuestions.answer: The status quo bias and loss aversion
quizQuestions.explanation: People tend to stick with default options due to the status quo bias and loss aversion — changing from a default feels like a loss.

quizQuestions.question: The social norm approach to reducing problematic drinking among students involves:
options: Correcting students' overestimates of peers' drinking by sharing accurate norms
Increasing the perceived cost of alcohol
quizQuestions.answer: Correcting students' overestimates of peers' drinking by sharing accurate norms
quizQuestions.explanation: The social norms intervention corrects exaggerated perceptions of peer drinking to reduce pluralistic ignorance and thereby reduce problem drinking.

quizQuestions.question: The 'nudge' approach to behavior change (Thaler & Sunstein) relies on:
options: Redesigning choice architecture to make desired behaviors the easy default
quizQuestions.answer: Redesigning choice architecture to make desired behaviors the easy default
quizQuestions.explanation: Nudge theory uses behavioral insights to design environments where the desired behavior is the path of least resistance, without restricting choice.

questions.question: The fundamental attribution error involves over-attributing others' behavior to their:
questions.answer: Dispositional (internal) characteristics
questions.explanation: The FAE leads us to explain others' behavior as reflecting their character, underweighting situational pressures.

questions.question: Normative social influence leads to conformity driven by:
options: The desire for social acceptance and approval
questions.answer: The desire for social acceptance and approval
questions.explanation: Normative influence is conformity to gain social approval, fit in, or avoid rejection.

questions.question: Cognitive dissonance is most likely when a behavior is:
options: Consistent with a strongly held attitude
Inconsistent with a freely chosen action or important belief
questions.answer: Inconsistent with a freely chosen action or important belief
questions.explanation: Cognitive dissonance is strongest when an important self-relevant belief is violated by a freely chosen behavior that is difficult to justify.

questions.question: In Asch's conformity experiments, the presence of one social supporter who agreed with the participant:
options: Increased conformity to the dissenter's view
questions.answer: Greatly reduced conformity
questions.explanation: A single ally broke the unanimity of the majority, dramatically reducing the participant's tendency to conform to the incorrect group answer.

questions.question: Social loafing is reduced when individual contributions are:
options: Small relative to the group's goal
questions.answer: Identifiable and evaluated
questions.explanation: When people know their individual contribution is being measured, they are less likely to social loaf.

questions.question: The self-serving bias involves attributing success to _____ and failure to _____.
questions.answer: Internal factors; external factors
questions.explanation: The self-serving bias protects self-esteem by crediting our own abilities for successes and blaming external circumstances for failures.

questions.question: The central route to persuasion is most effective when the audience:
options: Has low motivation and ability to process the message
Has high motivation and ability to evaluate arguments
Is highly susceptible to peripheral cues
questions.answer: Has high motivation and ability to evaluate arguments
questions.explanation: The central route involves careful evaluation of argument quality and is most effective when individuals are motivated and able to process the message.

questions.question: Milgram's obedience experiments showed that approximately ___% of participants went to the maximum shock level.
questions.explanation: Milgram's landmark finding: about 65% of participants followed orders to deliver what they believed were maximum 450V shocks.

questions.question: Groupthink is characterized by:
options: Vigorous internal debate and minority dissent
Suppression of dissent and illusion of invulnerability
questions.answer: Suppression of dissent and illusion of invulnerability
questions.explanation: Groupthink involves the suppression of critical thinking to preserve group cohesion, leading to overconfidence and poor decisions.

questions.question: The bystander effect is primarily caused by:
options: Lack of empathy in large groups
Diffusion of responsibility among multiple observers
Fear of personal injury when helping
questions.answer: Diffusion of responsibility among multiple observers
questions.explanation: The bystander effect occurs because each person assumes someone else will help, diluting any individual's felt responsibility.

questions.question: The mere exposure effect demonstrates that:
options: Repeated non-threatening exposure to a stimulus increases liking
Novelty is the primary driver of attraction
Exposure to a stimulus reduces its impact
questions.answer: Repeated non-threatening exposure to a stimulus increases liking
questions.explanation: Zajonc's mere exposure effect shows that simply being exposed to something more often increases positive feelings toward it.

questions.question: Tajfel's Social Identity Theory explains in-group bias as resulting from:
options: Realistic group conflict over scarce resources
Deriving self-esteem from group membership and favoring the in-group
questions.answer: Deriving self-esteem from group membership and favoring the in-group
questions.explanation: SIT proposes that people favor their in-group because doing so enhances the self-esteem derived from group membership.

questions.question: The actor-observer bias predicts that we explain our own behavior primarily in terms of:
questions.answer: The situation and context
questions.explanation: The actor-observer bias: as actors, we focus on situational factors; as observers, we focus on others' dispositions.

questions.question: The foot-in-the-door technique involves:
options: Making a very large request first, then a smaller one
Starting with a small request, then making a larger one
Using emotional threats to gain compliance
Offering a large reward for compliance
questions.answer: Starting with a small request, then making a larger one
questions.explanation: Foot-in-the-door: getting someone to agree to a small initial request increases their likelihood of agreeing to a larger subsequent request.

questions.question: The ABC model of attitudes consists of:
questions.answer: Affect, Behavior, Cognition
questions.explanation: Attitudes have three components: an emotional/evaluative component (Affect), a behavioral tendency (Behavior), and a belief/knowledge component (Cognition).

questions.question: The contact hypothesis (Allport) proposes that prejudice is reduced by contact that involves:
options: Any type of interaction between groups
Equal status, cooperative interaction under institutional support toward shared goals
Exposure to media representations of out-groups
questions.answer: Equal status, cooperative interaction under institutional support toward shared goals
questions.explanation: Allport identified key conditions: equal status, cooperative interdependence, institutional support, and common goals.

questions.question: Sternberg's 'companionate love' consists of which components?
questions.answer: Intimacy and commitment only
questions.explanation: Companionate love combines deep emotional closeness (intimacy) and long-term commitment but lacks intense physical passion.

questions.question: Social exchange theory predicts greatest relationship satisfaction when:
options: Rewards are maximized regardless of costs
Rewards exceed costs and alternatives are poor
questions.answer: Rewards exceed costs and alternatives are poor
questions.explanation: Social exchange theory: satisfaction depends on rewards minus costs relative to one's comparison level for alternatives (CLalt).

questions.question: The frustration-aggression hypothesis was revised (Berkowitz) to include the role of:
questions.answer: Cognitive appraisal and environmental cues
questions.explanation: Berkowitz revised the hypothesis to note that frustration only leads to aggression when negative affect is experienced and environmental cues prime aggressive responding.

questions.question: Diffusion of responsibility in the bystander effect means:
options: Bystanders actively decide not to help
Personal responsibility to help is diluted among multiple observers
Helping is delegated to the most capable bystander
Observers overestimate others' capacity to help
questions.answer: Personal responsibility to help is diluted among multiple observers
questions.explanation: Each bystander assumes someone else will take action, so the responsibility any individual feels to intervene decreases as group size increases.

questions.question: In-group favoritism and out-group derogation tend to arise even when groups are based on:
options: Minimal and arbitrary distinctions (minimal group paradigm)
questions.answer: Minimal and arbitrary distinctions (minimal group paradigm)
questions.explanation: Tajfel's minimal group studies showed that even arbitrary group distinctions are enough to trigger in-group favoritism.

questions.question: The door-in-the-face technique is based on the psychological principle of:
questions.answer: Reciprocal concessions
questions.explanation: When a requester backs down from a large initial request, the target feels obligated to reciprocate by conceding to the smaller follow-up request.

questions.question: Equity theory predicts that people who are over-benefited in a relationship will feel:
questions.answer: Guilty and uncomfortable
questions.explanation: Equity theory predicts distress for both under-benefited and over-benefited partners — receiving more than one gives can create guilt.

questions.question: Bandura's Bobo doll experiment demonstrated that aggression:
options: Can be learned by observing and imitating models
Only occurs in response to frustration
questions.answer: Can be learned by observing and imitating models
questions.explanation: Children who observed an adult model behave aggressively toward the Bobo doll subsequently imitated those aggressive behaviors.

questions.question: Stereotype threat refers to:
options: The fear of confirming a negative stereotype about one's group, which impairs performance
The tendency to apply stereotypes automatically
The threat that stereotypes pose to social harmony
questions.answer: The fear of confirming a negative stereotype about one's group, which impairs performance
questions.explanation: Steele and Aronson showed that when people are aware of a negative stereotype about their group, the anxiety of potentially confirming it can disrupt their performance.

questions.question: The scapegoating theory of prejudice proposes that prejudice:
options: Results from displaced aggression onto a vulnerable target group
Is driven purely by cognitive categorization
questions.answer: Results from displaced aggression onto a vulnerable target group
questions.explanation: Scapegoating proposes that frustrated individuals redirect aggression toward a safe, powerless target — typically a minority group.

questions.question: The 'what is beautiful is good' halo effect refers to the assumption that:
options: Physically attractive people also have positive personality traits
Beauty is universally defined across cultures
questions.answer: Physically attractive people also have positive personality traits
questions.explanation: The physical attractiveness halo effect leads us to infer that attractive people are also kinder, smarter, and more competent.

questions.question: Deindividuation is most likely to occur when people feel:
options: Anonymous and part of a large group
Strongly motivated to achieve a goal
questions.answer: Anonymous and part of a large group
questions.explanation: Anonymity and immersion in a group reduce self-awareness and self-regulation, increasing the likelihood of impulsive or normative behavior.

questions.question: The social norms intervention approach to reducing problem drinking corrects:
options: Underestimates of the health consequences of drinking
questions.answer: Overestimates of peers' drinking (pluralistic ignorance)
questions.explanation: Students typically overestimate how much peers drink; correcting this misperception reduces pluralistic ignorance and can lower individual drinking.

questions.question: Group polarization after discussion typically occurs because:
options: The group reaches a compromise between extremes
Discussion exposes members to persuasive arguments supporting the dominant view
A charismatic leader pushes the group toward extreme positions
questions.answer: Discussion exposes members to persuasive arguments supporting the dominant view
questions.explanation: Group discussion produces attitude extremity by generating more arguments in favor of the dominant prediscussion position, strengthening members' initial leanings.

questions.question: Agentic state theory (Milgram) explains obedience by proposing that:
options: People shift moral responsibility to the authority figure
Authority figures suppress critical thinking neurologically
Obedience is an innate social instinct
questions.answer: People shift moral responsibility to the authority figure
questions.explanation: In the agentic state, people act as agents of authority and transfer moral responsibility upward, reducing their own sense of culpability.

questions.question: Implicit Association Tests (IATs) measure:
options: The speed of associations between concepts, reflecting implicit attitudes
questions.answer: The speed of associations between concepts, reflecting implicit attitudes
questions.explanation: IATs measure the strength of automatic associations between target concepts and evaluative attributes using response time as an index of implicit bias.

questions.question: Social facilitation on difficult tasks predicts performance will:
options: Improve in the presence of observers
Be unaffected by the presence of others
Worsen in the presence of observers
questions.answer: Worsen in the presence of observers
questions.explanation: Social facilitation: arousal from being observed enhances performance on easy tasks but impairs performance on complex or novel tasks.

questions.question: Reciprocal liking in attraction research means:
options: We are attracted to people who are similar to us
We like people more when we know they like us
We prefer people we have spent more time with
We are attracted to people who are physically attractive
questions.answer: We like people more when we know they like us
questions.explanation: Reciprocity of liking is a powerful predictor of attraction — knowing someone likes us typically increases our liking of them.

questions.question: The 'nudge' approach to public policy uses insights from social psychology to:
options: Redesign choice environments to make beneficial behaviors easier defaults
Use fear appeals to motivate behavior change
Educate people about the consequences of their choices
questions.answer: Redesign choice environments to make beneficial behaviors easier defaults
questions.explanation: Nudge theory leverages behavioral economics and social psychology to design choice architectures that guide people toward beneficial decisions without restricting freedom.
